pullRequest.title: DOC: note in Comparison Table that np.ma is not implemented; suggest alternative
pullRequest.body:
This PR adds a note to the [Comparison Table](https://docs.cupy.dev/en/stable/reference/comparison.html#comparison-table) that CuPy does not implement NumPy's masked array interface, but that [MArray](https://github.com/mdhaber/marray) provides an alternative.

MArray-wrapped CuPy arrays are currently supported by almost all `scipy.stats` functions that support CuPy . For example:

```python3
from marray import cupy as xp
from scipy import stats
x = xp.asarray([1, 2, 3, 4])
y = xp.asarray([2, 3, 4, 5, 1000, xp.inf, xp.nan], 
               mask=[False]*4 + [True]*3)
res = stats.ttest_ind(x, y)
# TtestResult(statistic=MArray(array(-1.09544512), array(False)), 
#             pvalue=MArray(array(0.3153336), array(False)), 
#             df=MArray(array(6.), array(False)))
```
See SciPy's ["Support for the array API standard"](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/dev/api-dev/array_api.html#support-for-the-array-api-standard) page and ["Support on GPU"](https://scipy.github.io/devdocs/dev/api-dev/array_api_modules_tables/stats.html#support-on-gpu) table for more information about MArray and CuPy support in SciPy.

Closes gh-2225.

pullRequest.title: Implement `cupy.linalg.eig` / `cupy.linalg.eigvals`
pullRequest.body:
This closes #3255 and also closes #6359 .

This PR uses `cusolverDnXgeev` to solve non-hermitian eigenvalue problems on GPU.

pullRequest.title: Allow cupy.ndarray as repeats argument to cupy.repeat
pullRequest.body:
cupy.repeat now accepts a CuPy ndarray for the repeats parameter, matching NumPy's API. Scalar-like inputs (size-1 or 0-D arrays) use an efficient broadcast-copy kernel. Multi-element inputs use cumsum + searchsorted + take on the GPU. List/tuple inputs with len > 1, previously O(n * kernel_launch), now use the same GPU path.

Closes #3849. There are probably good reasons why this addition hasn't been done before (there _is_ a D2H transfer) given how long #3849 has been open for and how much interest there has been in it, so I'm curious to find out why! This feature has actually been on my wishlist for a few years too.

Claude Code (Opus 4.6) was used to assist in the development of this PR, but I was also heavily involved. These changes were actually spawned from my efforts to improve sparse matrix classes in #9825, and like in that PR I'm still doing my final human review of these changes.

pullRequest.title: Fix cuSOLVER feature/version detection for `eig` and `eigvals`
pullRequest.body:
Follows up #8980. Closes #5530.
* Fixes compilation & runtime failure on CUDA 12.6.0 and 12.6.1. Only load geev symbols on CUDA 12.6.2+ by using SoftLink.
* Removes unnecessary stubs as the minimum CUDA version supported is now CUDA 11.2.

pullRequest.title: Make `cuda.is_available()` guard against (almost) all errors
pullRequest.body:
Based on the issues, it seems best if `cuda.is_available()` can in practice never fail.
Since it seems to have no docs entry, added it to the CUDA docs section and some brief docs.

It is a bit unfortunate that one has to do random cupy calls for diagnosing problems, but it didn't seem very useful to add `is_available(show_failure=True)` or document that.

Closes gh-6702, gh-9091

(Sanity checked with `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=""`, although that error is of course the one that was unproblematic before!)

pullRequest.title: Migrate to pyproject.toml
pullRequest.body: Closes #6986. Related to #7577 .

pullRequest.title: BUG: Fix incorrect initialization in bspline kernel
pullRequest.body:
This fixes the use of uninitialized memory in the bspline kernel `d_boor` kernel.
Testing is unfortunately not really possible directly, this leads to occasionally test failures.

I did test it locally, by replacing all ``empty`` calls with either filling with NaN or the minimum integer, though.

Closes gh-7240

---

I am not really worried about it being wrong (the original code always uses uninitialized data here), but maybe @andfoy you can have a brief look? In some paths it seems it only matters that this value isn't NaN or similar (i.e. probably it leads to `value - value` which is mathematically be zero but isn't for NaN/inf).
But I think the design-matrix tests wanted one here?

pullRequest.title: Make `cupyx.scipy` submodule imports lazy
pullRequest.body:
closes https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/8336

Two changes:

First, formerly `import cupyx` eagerly imported `cupyx.scipy.fft`, `linalg` and `special`. This led to strange effects downstream: `import cupyx` made `cupyx.scipy.special` available but not `cupyx.scipy.ndimage`.
Fix this by moving corresponding imports from the module level to call sites.

Second, implement a module `__getattr__` for `cupyx.scipy`, so that for all cupyx.scipy submodules the following works:

```
>>> import cupyx
>>> cupyx.scipy.ndimage.convolve
<function convolve at 0x7f4445220c20>
```

pullRequest.title: BUG: Make sure indexing routines are OK with huge arrays
pullRequest.body:
Many of these routines used int32 for some shapes internally.
Usually, that works out just fine, since all of these shapes
represent only a part of the array, it is unlikely to be larger
than an int32. But it very much can be larger.

Closes gh-7482

---

~I admit, my env got broken somehow and one of the tests didn't work, but I confirmed it was broken at least.~ (fixed in a new env)

I saw some similar tests that said ROCm doesn't support these large indices?  I wasn't sure if that is still valid, nor am I sure if the `MemoryError` try/except is helpful or doesn't matter.

pullRequest.title: Remove deprecated `nvrtc.getNVVM` API
pullRequest.body: Close #7804.

pullRequest.title: ENH,BUG: Allow strides without mem, fix empty byte-bounds
pullRequest.body:
It ended up a bit more than I thought... on the plus side:
* Speed up creation slightly by avoiding use of Python functions for the bounds calculation.
* Use cython side helper for byte-bounds, which also fixes a bug (NumPy 2 moved this function, but a different thing.)
* Allow allocation if `strides=` is passed, closes gh-7818 (Limited to what NumPy does, whether thought through or not...)

All of these are very small improvements, but since I started, let's finish it I guess.

pullRequest.title: Fix #7828: Remove `self` from ndarray docstring signature
pullRequest.body:
## Summary
Remove `self` from the `__init__` signature in `cupy.ndarray` docstring.

## Problem
The docstring contained `__init__(self, shape, ...)`, which Sphinx rendered as:
`class cupy.ndarray(self, shape, dtype=float, ...)`

The correct rendering (as in v10.6.0) should be:
`class cupy.ndarray(shape, dtype=float, ...)`

Fixes #7828

pullRequest.title: Support CUDA wheels
pullRequest.body:
Close #8013.

This PR adds `cuda-pathfinder` as a dependency to CuPy's CUDA builds, and use it to load needed CUDA libraries. Turns out this is fairly easy: We just need to inject a pathfinder call prior to importing the Python extension module that offers the bindings to the C library. Then, the pathfinder will first look for possible locations of CUDA wheels, if installed. This allows us to support CUDA wheels. The needed CUDA component wheels can be installed optionally via ~~`[all]`~~ `[ctk]`.

It is not clear to me if any of the library loading helpers in `cupy/_environment.py` needs any changes or removal. My local test shows they can stay and do not interfere with the pathfinder.

A separate PR to the release tool implements the actual dependency injection: https://github.com/cupy/cupy-release-tools/pull/466. The CuPy sdist and ROCm builds are not affected.

pullRequest.title: Improve pathfinder support
pullRequest.body:
Close #9501. Close #9537. Close #8604.

Below is an LLM-generated summary.

This pull request introduces significant improvements to how CuPy locates and loads CUDA libraries and headers. The main changes involve integrating the `cuda-pathfinder` utility for more robust and accurate library discovery, simplifying library loading to avoid duplicate loads, and improving support for conda-based CUDA installations. Additionally, the runtime information reporting has been enhanced to include library paths. These changes aim to make CuPy more reliable across different environments and reduce issues with mismatched or missing CUDA libraries.

**Library Loading and Discovery Improvements**
- Transitioned dynamic library loading (e.g., for `cudart`, `cufft`, `cusolver`, `cusparse`, `nvrtc`) to use `cuda-pathfinder`, passing library handles directly to avoid loading the same library multiple times and risking version mismatches. The `SoftLink` class now accepts a handle and uses it to attach to already-loaded libraries. (`cupy_backends/cuda/_softlink.pyx`, `cupy_backends/cuda/api/_runtime_softlink.pxi`, `cupy_backends/cuda/libs/_cnvrtc.pxi`, `cupy_backends/cuda/libs/cusolver.pyx`, `cupy_backends/cuda/libs/cusparse.pyx`, `cupy/cuda/cufft.pyx`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-cbc27dbc38858d936362f3f65b9e090ff6192afedaf00dd14b899b1bf4963257L14-R31) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-6cb8381aee6141f41efe652d4a194c261c3bf38092c14a931303bc3b5d985786R36-R48) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-08d1fbf5fd5501384a37161816ad3b3b2b042370c5bb8a278d7eaa9ad6bc6939R119-R126) [[4]](diffhunk://#diff-08d1fbf5fd5501384a37161816ad3b3b2b042370c5bb8a278d7eaa9ad6bc6939L165-R150) [[5]](diffhunk://#diff-16ac314758c2277313ec2eaccc5fe6eeae81086bfaa8f1a0df3b9ee83384a975L1007-R1018) [[6]](diffhunk://#diff-30585ad4d0aa93771c86e3f3d1b49d77a7f9bd2f0e83e6f48188d5f09bfeab7dL1374-R1389) [[7]](diffhunk://#diff-f5ad605b6a808958307974d6802c4b960d424dcab7948ffc026105da57237a8eR54-R74)

- Updated the CUDA environment detection logic to use `cuda-pathfinder` for locating libraries and headers, and improved conda environment support by detecting the correct CUDA toolkit path within conda installations. (`cupy/_environment.py`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-ceab17c630debe3694ad66bda62640f510a6b91b614cbc0870134284766ff2bcR114-R171) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-ceab17c630debe3694ad66bda62640f510a6b91b614cbc0870134284766ff2bcR380-R395) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-ceab17c630debe3694ad66bda62640f510a6b91b614cbc0870134284766ff2bcL520-R604)

**Header and Include Path Handling**
- Refactored header search logic to prefer conda-installed headers and use `cuda-pathfinder` as a fallback, improving reliability in environments where CUDA is installed via conda or wheels. (`cupy/_environment.py`)

**Runtime and Diagnostic Enhancements**
- Extended runtime information reporting to include the absolute paths of loaded CUDA libraries, improving diagnostics and user visibility into which libraries are being used. (`cupyx/_runtime.py`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-1b1d40d41a8e985c283bcf724bab26902e2837c3cbf0ccca07a90e8e0b643381R16-R17) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-1b1d40d41a8e985c283bcf724bab26902e2837c3cbf0ccca07a90e8e0b643381R30-R48) [[3]](diffhunk://#diff-1b1d40d41a8e985c283bcf724bab26902e2837c3cbf0ccca07a90e8e0b643381R87) [[4]](diffhunk://#diff-1b1d40d41a8e985c283bcf724bab26902e2837c3cbf0ccca07a90e8e0b643381R96-R133) [[5]](diffhunk://#diff-1b1d40d41a8e985c283bcf724bab26902e2837c3cbf0ccca07a90e8e0b643381R167-R168)

**Miscellaneous**
- Added `cusparselt` to the list of supported submodules and ensured correct mapping for library loading. (`cupy_backends/cuda/libs/__init__.py`)
- Updated NVCC path selection logic to avoid using NVCC in HIP environments. (`cupy/_core/_cub_reduction.pyx`)
- Applied minor code cleanups and optimizations, such as adding functools imports and caching. (`cupy/_environment.py`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-ceab17c630debe3694ad66bda62640f510a6b91b614cbc0870134284766ff2bcL6-R7) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-ceab17c630debe3694ad66bda62640f510a6b91b614cbc0870134284766ff2bcR576)

pullRequest.title: Add N-D array support to upfirdn
pullRequest.body:
Extend cupyx.scipy.signal.upfirdn to support N-dimensional arrays, matching SciPy's behavior. Previously limited to 1D/2D arrays.

Implementation:
- Extract 2D kernel code into _apply_filter_2d helper method
- For N-D arrays: move target axis to end, reshape to 2D, apply existing kernel, reshape back to original dimensions

Ref #8716

pullRequest.title: Fix illegal memory access in LinearNDInterpolator
pullRequest.body:
Close https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/8933.

`nextNearest` can become `-1` when the all opposites of `nearest` are visited. This PR stops the search for the closest points and concludes that there is no triangle covering the query point in such a case.
cc: @andfoy

pullRequest.title: Fix intersect1d crash with empty arrays
pullRequest.body:
Fix illegal memory access when either input array is empty. The CUDA kernel was accessing non-existent memory, corrupting the CUDA context for subsequent operations.

Add early return with proper dtype handling using result_type() to match NumPy behavior.

Fixes #8937

pullRequest.title: CUDA 11.1 or earlier is no longer supported
pullRequest.body:
This is the fix for Issue [#9074](https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/9074).

Since cupy no longer supports CUDA 11.1 or earlier, the #if checks from [cupy/cupyx/scipy/signal/_upfirdn.py](https://github.com/cupy/cupy/blob/53127099fe5c441fec97567f57974504342f304d/cupyx/scipy/signal/_upfirdn.py#L62) and [cupy/cupyx/scipy/signal/_upfirdn.py](https://github.com/cupy/cupy/blob/53127099fe5c441fec97567f57974504342f304d/cupyx/scipy/signal/_upfirdn.py#L175) have been removed.

pullRequest.title: ENH,MAINT: Allow cupy-like protocols in setitem and consolidate
pullRequest.body:
This allows `cupy_arr[...] = cupy_like` where cupy-like supports the CUDA array interface or `__cupy_get_ndarray__`.

Since there were a few (not many!) places that have a similar logic I, opionatedly, consolidated them into a `cdef inline` function. Similarly moved the test helper classes to not have to repeat them.

The biggest part of the diff is just moving those helpers!

I am very sure that `cdef inline` must be in the `.pyd` file to actually be inlined (which means many are currenlty not).

Closes gh-9089

pullRequest.title: [nccl] add nccl comm split
pullRequest.body:
# Target
This PR aims to add commSplit support to cupy and close #9096 

# Implementation:
Given color, key and optional nccl_config, a new communicator will be created based on an old larger one. It's only supported if nccl version >= 2.18.1

```python
from cupy.cuda import nccl
comm = nccl.NcclCommunicator(world_size, uid, rank)
new_comm = comm.commSplit(color, key)
if new_comm is not None:
    # use new_comm for collective communication
    new_comm.destroy()
comm.destroy()
```

Fix a math error in `expm` implementation, where a formula from the paper was incorrectly translated from math to python.

pullRequest.title: feat: make `cupy.nan_to_num` broadcast `nan`, `posinf`, and `neginf` kwargs
pullRequest.body:
Fixes https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/9143

Following numpy here: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/3ccbc6c29bb56795d32ee685bccdc1e9ca8ae47f/numpy/lib/_type_check_impl.py#L469-L492,
I don't think that cupy should check for `cupy.isnan(x)` and `cupy.isinf(x)` for the user defined arguments `nan`, `posinf`, and `neginf`. Numpy doesn't care about them, and does the `copyto` anyway. I believe that `cupy.asanyarray(x, dtype)` is enough here.

cc @leofang since we went over this at the SciPy sprints.

https://setuptools.pypa.io/en/latest/userguide/datafiles.html

> To further include them into the wheels, you can use the include_package_data keyword:
> ...
> The default value for `tool.setuptools.include-package-data` is `true` when projects are configured via `pyproject.toml`. This behaviour differs from `setup.cfg` and `setup.py` (where `include_package_data` is False by default)

pullRequest.title: Avoid allocating an intermediate array in `cupy.random.choice`
pullRequest.body:
Turns out we can't use `cuda::shuffle_iterator` or `thrust::shuffle_iterator` for a few reasons:
1. They are not available in CCCL 3.1.2 that we just updated to
2. Using them as-is is challenging because
   - We need pre-compiled kernels + C/Cython bindings (also noted in the inline comment)
   - We need to figure out a way to influence its RNG there
3. Both have serious bugs that just got fixed upstream (I propagated the fixes to here)
   - uniform randomness
   - cipher bit initialization

The solution here is arguably very simple: We initialize the state (a POD struct) on host and pass it to the device as a kernel argument. Then, the bijection device function can be invoked straightforwardly.

pullRequest.title: Update to CCCL v3.1.2
pullRequest.body:
All diffs are explained by the excellent [CCCL 3.0 Migration Guide](https://nvidia.github.io/cccl/cccl/3.0_migration_guide.html):
- CUB SpMV is removed. 
   - The CCCL team and the cuSPARSE team suggested that cuSPARSE should be as performant if not more than CCCL, as a result the CUB version has been removed in CCCL v3.0 ([link](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cccl/blob/65050fef2ab8bf10e0d2b053ea6481efea72292c/docs/cccl/3.0_migration_guide.rst?plain=1#L107)).
- `FpLimits` is removed in favor of `cuda::std::numeric_limits` 
- `<thrust/optional>` is removed in favor of `cuda::std::optional` 
- ``cub::TransformInputIterator``: Use ``thrust::transform_iterator`` instead
- ``cub::CountingInputIterator``: Use ``thrust::counting_iterator`` instead
- `cub::{Sum,Min,Max}` are replaced by `cuda::std::plus` and `cuda::{minimum,maximum}`
- Bump C++ standard everywhere (I think!) to C++17 due to CCCL 3.0 requirement

Additional changes:
- `atomicAdd_block` is needed by CUB's recent histogram change ([link](https://github.com/NVIDIA/cccl/pull/4973/))

pullRequest.title: Fix cuTENSOR workspace size query
pullRequest.body:
closes #9350

This MR updates cutensor contraction backend with improvement on the workspace size inquiry. 

An additional call to `cutensorPlanGetAttribute` is made to reduce the workspace size actually needed to execute the contraction. A few extra bindings were added to support this improvement. Example usage from [here](https://github.com/NVIDIA/CUDALibrarySamples/blob/a2433ef214c238e0abdb5e4307ba4b1862f81d1a/cuTENSOR/contraction.cu#L286)

pullRequest.title: Fix tf2sos failing for constant transfer function.
pullRequest.body:
Closes #9376

This PR makes `tf2sos` work when a constant transfer function is passed. The problem occurred due to array construction in `zpk2sos` which assumes that the system gain `k` is a float and fails when `k` is a 0d CuPy array. To fix this, I added a check for if `k` is a 0d CuPy array, and call `k.get()` to convert to a 0d NumPy array, which does work for such array construction. I made it check specifically for a 0d CuPy array to try to make the error message less confusing if a higher dimensional CuPy array is passed for `k`, e.g. to avoid an error message referring to NumPy arrays.

I've also copied the tests for `tf2sos` over from SciPy, including the ones that failed due to #9376.

pullRequest.title: Change nccl get_unique_id to return a bytes string
pullRequest.body:
This simplifies things slightly, but mostly avoids any sensitivity to systems with different char signedness.
I.e. casting the `char` to an integer tuple means we get negative values on many platforms (e.g. intel/AMD CPUs) while ARM system use unsigned char and would give signed values.

Using a bytes string, means that we can exchange that directly in the communicator and don't need to do the (incorrect) `+128` (rather than a cast to unsigned int).

Since CuPy v14 is a major version and since nothing should change for most use-cases this seems sensible to try to just change to me.

Closes gh-9435, gh-9430

(I should probably to confirm this on an ARM system, although if anything the systems would fail probably and Cython generates `memcpy` and `FromBytesAndSize`, so it should be safe.)

pullRequest.title: DOC: Remove experimental on async allocator
pullRequest.body: Small doc change to remove experimental notes from async allocator and close gh-9448, since that seems to have agreement.

pullRequest.title: File cache: use os.replace (clarity) and accept PermissionError
pullRequest.body:
This uses `os.replace()` since it is a bit more clear that a within-folder move should be atomic.
It also allows `PermissionError` on windows since it is possible that the file was already created by another thread and opened for use and windows will then raise a `PermissionError` when the replace fails.

Unfortunately, I am not sure there is a good way to test this.

Closes gh-9463

pullRequest.title: Fix `ctk` extras
pullRequest.body:
This PR fixes two problems:

* `cudart` was missing from dependencies. We need `nvidia-cuda-runtime` package because we use CTK headers to compile kernels. This fixes #9547.
* Fix `ctk` extras not declared when building CuPy from source with UV (both `uv pip install 'cupy[ctk]'` and `uv pip install -e '.[ctk]' --no-build-isolation` was not working). Binary packages (e.g. `uv pip install cupy-cuda13x[ctk]`) or Pip users (e.g. `pip install cupy`) are unaffected.

pullRequest.title: Fix #9568: Handle negative inputs in sawtooth and square waveforms
pullRequest.body:
## Summary
- Fix incorrect behavior of `cupyx.scipy.signal.sawtooth` and `cupyx.scipy.signal.square` for negative input values
- The root cause was the difference between C's `fmod()` and Python's `%` operator:
  - C `fmod(-0.5, 2π)` returns `-0.5` (same sign as dividend)
  - Python `-0.5 % (2π)` returns positive value (always non-negative)
- Added normalization to ensure `tmod` is always in `[0, 2π)` range

## Test plan
- [x] Verified fix in Google Colab with GPU
- [x] Added tests for negative input values (`test_sawtooth_negative_input`, `test_square_negative_input`)
- [] CI tests

Closes #9568

pullRequest.title: Fix `TestChoiceReplaceFalse` check
Avoid caching cuFFT legacy callback artifacts
pullRequest.body: Close #9666.

pullRequest.title: Fix: relax dtype check in views (including zero-copy array constructors)
pullRequest.body:
Close #9709.

I am not really happy about this fix, because it relaxes the nice check that @seberg recently added. But it seems if we are to support CAI there is no other way, since CAI uses NumPy dtypes which brings in a large surface of APIs that we have accidentally supported for many years... 🥲

pullRequest.title: Always query hipcc for include directories in hiprtc
pullRequest.body:
hiprtc doesn't reliably include the correct system include directories on its own. Previously, we only queried hipcc when ROCM_HOME was not set, and had a version-specific workaround for ROCm 4.3.

This change simplifies the logic by always querying hipcc for include directories, which is more robust across different ROCm versions and configurations. This also helps to enable ROCm 7.2

pullRequest.title: MAINT: add missing names to linalg.__all__
pullRequest.body: fixes https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/9749

pullRequest.title: BUG: Fix regression for 32bit index flag in `.real` and broadcast
pullRequest.body:
I suspect I misunderstood the flag, as the offset is used to calculate the pointer offset as well and not just for index manipulation.

Adding a few tests, I noticed that broadcasting also got things wrong, one could also proof it via something like:
```
arr = cp.broadcast_arrays(cp.random.randint(255, size=2**30+100, dtype=cp.uint8), cp.empty((2, 1)))[0]
cp.put(arr, cp.arange(arr.size//2, arr.size), 1)
print(arr)
```

I audited places for `._view()` and `_set_shape_and_strides()`, but I am a bit wondering whether it this couldn't use a bigger refactor

Maybe I should refactor this into a flag to `_view` and `_set_shape_and_strides`?

Closes gh-9750

pullRequest.title: Fix DistributedArray missing NotImplementedError overrides for `mdspan` and `mT`
pullRequest.body: Close https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/9773.

pullRequest.title: Update `test_assumed_runtime_version` for Windows + CUDA >=13.0
pullRequest.body: Close cupy/cupy#9776.

pullRequest.title: BUG: Fix incomplete size guard for CUB segmented reduce and scan
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Minimal fix for #9780 — an alternative to the broader `int` → `size_t` rewrite in #9810.

The existing guard in `can_use_device_segmented_reduce` only checked `contiguous_size <= INT32_MAX` (per-segment size), but the C++ offset iterator in `cupy_cub.cu` computes `segment_size * segment_index` using `int`. When the **product** exceeds `INT32_MAX`, the `int` overflows and causes an illegal memory access.

For example, `cp.zeros((40000, 400, 200), dtype='f').sum(axis=2)`:
- `contiguous_size` = 200 → passes the old guard
- `n_segments` = 16,000,000 → fits in `int`
- But offset = 200 × 16,000,000 = 3,200,000,000 → **overflows `int`**

### Changes

1. **`can_use_device_segmented_reduce`**: Check `x.size <= 0x7fffffff` instead of `contiguous_size <= 0x7fffffff`. Since `x.size = contiguous_size × n_segments`, this covers the segment size, segment count, and their product in the offset iterator.

2. **`cub_scan`**: Add a missing size guard. `device_scan` casts `x.size` to `int` without checking, which silently truncates for arrays > `INT32_MAX`.

3. **Tests**: Large-array correctness tests adapted from #9810 (co-authored by @seberg), with a typo fix (`"first-part"` → `"first_part"` so the first_part test branch actually runs its assertions).

### Why not the `int` → `size_t` approach (#9810)?

CUB's tuning system has separate specializations for 32-bit vs 64-bit offset types (see `tuning_reduce.cuh` in CCCL). Passing `size_t` causes CUB to instantiate 64-bit offset kernels **for all problem sizes**, not just large ones. This means:
- Different vectorized load policies (e.g. `items_per_vec_load = 1` vs `2` on SM100)
- Higher register pressure (~25% more registers per thread, per CCCL profiling data)
- Potential perf regression across all reduction sizes, not just the large ones being fixed

This minimal fix instead falls back to CuPy's generic reduction kernel for oversized arrays, preserving the 32-bit CUB fast path for all sizes that fit.

### Test plan

- [ ] Existing CUB reduction tests pass (no behavior change for arrays ≤ `INT32_MAX`)
- [ ] Arrays with total size > `INT32_MAX` fall back gracefully (no crash, correct results via generic kernel)
- [x] Large-array correctness tests from #9810 included (marked `@testing.slow`, require 2+ GB GPU memory)
- [x] Fixed test typo from #9810: `"first-part"` → `"first_part"` so the first_part branch actually runs assertions

Closes #9780

-- Leo's bot

pullRequest.title: CI: Make sure local cache is warmed up at job start time
pullRequest.body: Close #9791.

pullRequest.title: 9804: Prevent hypergeometric infinite loops and other consequences of invalid inputs
pullRequest.body:
Should fix https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/9804 and also https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/9239.

Context: If `nsample > (ngood + nbad)` and `nsample > 10` we would end up in an infinite loop. Good news is that that's invalid input, and numpy just checks this case and some others before running the kernel.

I basically tried to port numpy's checks here. Added both Python-level and kernel level input validation for defense in depth, but I'm happy to change the approach though. I understand raising validation exceptions from Python at that point will sync the device, but given that this only happens on invalid input, I think it's ok?

Full disclosure: I had claude (opus 4.6) write the test cases. I think they look pretty good and match the overall style, but happy to tweak!

**EDIT(seberg): Marked as no-compat, since the random stream could change here, just for a brief note in release notes.**

pullRequest.title: Fix ZeroDivisionError when sorting along zero-length axis (#9816)
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #9816.

Also, optimize to not sort when axis is length 1; this avoids a kernel launch

pullRequest.title: BUG: fix cupy.interp returning nan at exact knot when fp contains inf
pullRequest.body:
## Problem

`cupy.interp` returns `nan` when a query point exactly equals a knot in `xp` and the adjacent value in `fp` is `inf`. NumPy returns the exact knot value correctly.

```python
import numpy as np
import cupy as cp

x, xp, fp = [2.0], [1, 2, 3, 4], [1, 2, np.inf, 4]
np.interp(x, xp, fp)   # [2.]  ✓
cp.interp(cp.array(x), cp.array(xp), cp.array(fp))  # [nan]  ✗
```

Fixes #9823.

## Root cause

The interpolation kernel computes:

```c
slope = (fy[x_idx+1] - fy[x_idx]) / (fx[x_idx+1] - fx[x_idx]);
out   = slope * (x[i] - fx[x_idx]) + fy[x_idx];
```

When `x[i] == fx[x_idx]` (exact lower-knot hit) and `fy[x_idx+1] == inf`:
- `slope = inf`
- `x[i] - fx[x_idx] = 0`
- `inf * 0 = nan` (IEEE 754)

The existing NaN-recovery fallback tries the upper-knot formula, which also produces `nan` in this case.

## Fix

Short-circuit to `fy[x_idx]` when `x[i] == fx[x_idx]`, avoiding the slope calculation entirely:

```c
if (x[i] == fx[x_idx]) {
    y = fy[x_idx];
} else {
    // existing slope calculation
}
```

## Test

Added `test_interp_inf_fy_at_knot` to `tests/cupy_tests/math_tests/test_misc.py`, covering all float `dtype_x` and float/complex `dtype_y` combinations.

pullRequest.title: sparse: work around cuSPARSE SpMM gridDim.y overflow (#9850)
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #9850.

cuSPARSE csrmm_alg1 sets gridDim.y = ceil(n/16) where n is the number of columns in the dense output. When n > 65535*16 the grid dimension exceeds the hardware limit and the kernel silently produces zeros. Chunk the dense matrix along columns on CUDA < 13.1 where the bug is present.

I've confirmed the cuSPARSE fix is present on a system with `_runtime.runtimeGetVersion()` of 13020, but I'm unsure in which exact version it became available.

pullRequest.title: Implement `ndarray.byteswap()`
pullRequest.body:
Closes https://github.com/cupy/cupy/issues/9854

I'd really appreciate feedback. My idea was to write kernels using byte permutation that I kinda stole from https://github.com/NVIDIA/cccl/blob/c0121ac78e03fab778d4a30b2bbb8e4b82bcbfdc/libcudacxx/include/cuda/std/__bit/byteswap.h which you vendor under `third_party`.
For complex numbers, I thought swapping the real and imaginary parts individually instead of an explicit kernel would be fine.
